critic_raw:
This multimodal neural network vs. logistic regression experiment for post-operative complication prediction has a solid temporal split structure and appropriate use of sensitivity at fixed specificity as the primary clinical metric. However, the design has meaningful methodological shortcomings.

The dataset construction begins with all patients undergoing orthopedic surgery, then selects those with documented complications balanced against uncomplicated cases. The phrase 'balance these against uncomplicated cases' implies deliberate case-control sampling or artificial undersampling of the majority class. If the class distribution is artificially balanced in the training data but not in the test data (which should reflect natural prevalence), then models trained on balanced data and evaluated on natural-prevalence test data will produce miscalibrated probabilities, undermining the clinical interpretation of predicted risk. This is a fundamental design flaw that should be addressed explicitly.

The multimodal neural network includes clinical text embeddings (frozen pretrained embeddings from SciBERT or BioBERT) while the logistic regression baseline uses only structured features. This is the same confound seen in similar studies: architecture and modality are conflated. A logistic regression with text TF-IDF features would help isolate the architectural contribution from the multimodal information access.

Stratification within temporal windows by surgeon is described as a confound control, but surgeon identifier is also included as a fixed effect feature in both models. If the same surgeon appears in training, validation, and test sets (which is likely in a 5-year single-site dataset), then surgeon identity in the test set is not out-of-sample. This means the surgeon fixed effect acts as a quasi-look-up table for surgeon-specific complication rates, inflating test performance beyond what would generalize to new surgeons or new sites.

The primary metric—sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity—is chosen on the validation set, but the threshold selected on validation may not correspond to 85% specificity on the test set due to score distribution shifts. The design should specify how the threshold will be recalibrated or confirmed on test data, or alternatively report sensitivity across the full specificity range.
all_issues_raised: Artificial class balancing in dataset construction creates miscalibrated probabilities when test set reflects natural prevalence
Multimodal model vs. structured-only logistic regression conflates modality access with architecture; text-augmented logistic regression ablation is absent
Surgeon fixed effect as a feature in a single-site dataset allows models to memorize surgeon-specific rates rather than learn generalizable clinical predictors
Threshold selected for 85% specificity on validation may not transfer to test set due to score distribution shifts; no recalibration strategy described
5-year single-site orthopedic dataset may exhibit practice drift (changes in surgical protocols, complication definitions) that is not addressed as a temporal confound
Sensitivity at 85% specificity threshold set on validation set may not achieve 85% specificity on test set due to temporal distribution shift between validation and test periods
Frozen pretrained clinical language model embeddings may not capture operative-specific terminology — no evaluation of whether text features provide discriminative signal
Surgeon stratification does not account for new surgeons in the test period who have no training data and may have systematically different outcome patterns
Composite complication endpoint aggregates heterogeneous events with different risk factors — model may learn patterns of the most common complication and underperform on rarer outcomes
No analysis of whether complication types are equally represented across temporal windows, or whether case-mix shift in complication types affects the fairness of temporal comparison
Artificial case-control balancing produces miscalibrated probability outputs not reflective of natural complication prevalence; no recalibration protocol described
Surgeon identifier as fixed effect creates unseen category problem for temporal splits; mapping to 'other' collapses surgeons with different skill levels
Sensitivity at 85% specificity threshold derived from validation set may not transfer to test set if complication prevalence differs between validation and test windows
Frozen pretrained clinical LM embeddings may not represent institution-specific operative note terminology; no ablation tests whether text branch helps or harms the fusion
No discussion of whether 90-day complication outcome labels are complete for patients discharged near the end of the study window (months 51-60 have incomplete 90-day follow-up within the dataset)
